chapters.title: When the world shakes, truth comes out
chapters.text:
Before 1986, almost no one had heard of Ukraine, a large Eastern European country, or of Chornobyl, a Ukrainian city that housed the Chornobyl power plant. On that fateful day in 1986, the whole world changed. The tragedy, with its huge global impact, was bound to make people stop and wonder where we are going as a society.

If you visit the Chornobyl exclusion zone through the official tour today, you will absorb about as much radiation as an airplane passenger on a one-hour flight. But on April 26, the blast released the equivalent of five hundred Hiroshima bombs, throwing no more than five percent of the exploded reactor's nuclear fuel into the air. Imagine what could have happened if the other reactors had exploded in a chain reaction. Perhaps none of us would be alive today. Even now, more than thirty years after the Chornobyl tragedy, we are still dealing with the damage.

Learning the truth about history matters more than almost anything, because it shapes our everyday lives.

Historians still wrestle with the question of why this disaster happened. The real answer lies in the broken system of the Soviet Union. The USSR did everything it could to hide the actual scale of the catastrophe and its consequences, because its leaders knew they were to blame.

Although the days of the Soviet era are long behind us, the threat of a similar disaster still hangs over us. That is why we have to learn from the painful lessons of Chornobyl.

In this summary, we will pull back the Iron Curtain and discover what really happened on April 26, 1986.

The power plant is often called Chernobyl, but Chornobyl is the preferred Ukrainian transliteration.

chapters.title: Politics never sits still when people are around
chapters.text:
To fully understand what happened at Chornobyl, you have to look deeper than the surface and further back than the day the tragedy unfolded. The story of the Chornobyl catastrophe began on February 25, 1986, the day of the Communist Party Congress. About five thousand delegates gathered in Moscow to discuss and agree on the country's new development path. Mikhail Gorbachev, the new communist leader, and his newly appointed colleagues were eager to bring back the country's prosperity.

The keyword of the new development plan was acceleration. Soviet leaders believed the country needed a strong push through technological and scientific progress. Expanding the USSR's nuclear potential was part of that plan.

Among the special Ukrainian delegates at this Congress was Viktor Briukhanov, director of the Chornobyl Nuclear Power Station. Appointed to Chornobyl back in 1970, he had built the power station from scratch in the small city of Pripyat, providing jobs and homes to thousands of Soviet workers. Briukhanov was exhausted after fifteen years of nonstop work and unhappy after hearing some of Gorbachev's suggestions. The Communist Party dreamed of producing even more nuclear energy, and the ambitious plan was to build six more reactors at Chornobyl. Briukhanov had no choice but to agree.

You only have to read Gorbachev's Congress speech to understand why the Soviet Union was in such a hurry to produce more nuclear power. He was openly more concerned with atomic weapons than with nuclear energy. His goal, then, was not to give the country cheaper and better energy but to compete with the United States in the nuclear arms race. The Soviet appetite for nuclear power was frighteningly huge.

Doing things for the wrong reasons will always backfire. Doing something for the right reason is the key to success.

The reactor type used at Chornobyl was the RBMK, the High Power Channel Reactor. The developers were so confident in the model's safety that they proposed installing it on Red Square. In hindsight, it is a good thing that no one took them up on the offer.

chapters.title: Speed and quality rarely share a room
chapters.text:
Eager to leave his mark on the industry, Anatolii Maiorets, the new minister of energy and electrification, suggested that the USSR cut the time to build new reactors from seven years to five. This new agenda put unnecessary strain on the Chornobyl Power Station managers and the construction workers, creating a host of challenges.

Around that time, a local Pripyat reporter named Liubov Kovalevskaia published a special issue about the problems of working at the nuclear plant. Her findings were terrifying. About seventy percent of the hardware materials had serious defects, and some essential parts had not even arrived to finish proper construction. Those issues, combined with the crazy deadlines, made the production of every block and reactor impossible to complete on time. Meanwhile, Communist Party superiors ignored the situation and refused to see it as a challenge.

Complaints about difficulties with the construction of the new units were all but ignored at the top, as Serhii Plokhy has written.

The Chornobyl RBMK reactors not only lacked complete safety testing, they were also built too quickly and with defective materials, a recipe for disaster.

Some workers and journalists tried to share what they knew about these poor decisions, but their voices never reached the officials in charge. The high standards of construction that had been set up before no longer mattered. Meeting deadlines ranked higher than meeting safety requirements.

Disorganization and lack of accountability weaken established standards and personal responsibility.

In one of her more detailed reports on the construction process, journalist Kovalevskaia sharply criticized the whole project. She believed the government was wrong about how it was rebuilding the country's economy. Despite the danger of being expelled from the party, she kept spreading the truth. No one paid attention to what she had to say until April 26, 1986, when it was far too late.

The city of Chornobyl takes its name from a wormwood shrub. It directly references the biblical story about a wormwood star that supposedly fell into a river and poisoned many people.

chapters.title: Human error is almost written into the script
chapters.text:
April 25, 1986, marked the start of a long-awaited weekend. Some Pripyat residents planned to go hiking, work in their gardens, or enjoy unseasonably warm days. It was the beginning of a May holiday stretch and the last chance to get a few things done before the break.

The Chornobyl plant was running according to schedule, with no accidents or malfunctions. The plant's staff prepared to shut down Unit Four for routine system checks, tests, and repairs. The scheduled shutdown, however, began much later than planned.

Even machines are liable to error, because humans created them.

Despite the manufacturer's ban on doing it, the staff had to run the test because of the tight deadlines.

At first, they delayed the test because one of the chief engineers, Anatolii Diatlov, was absent. The delay stretched so long that a few shift changes took place. When Diatlov and the shift leaders finally started the test, they ran it at a dangerously low power level, two hundred megawatts. No one knows who started the test or why they took such an obvious risk. As the test began, several emergency signals sounded, but the staff ignored them.

The test lasted about thirty-six seconds, a span that later turned out to mark the difference between life and death for those involved. When it ended, one of the staff workers pressed the button to insert the control rods and shut down the reactor completely. That action backfired.

Already destabilized, the reaction levels began to rise rapidly. People in the control room heard a sudden noise, after which the ground started to shake. It felt like an earthquake but was far deadlier. The blast was so powerful that the reactor's two-hundred-ton upper biological shield blew through the roof. It landed back onto the reactor but left an opening through which radiation poured into the atmosphere. No one inside the fourth unit could have imagined what had just happened. Two fishermen at the nearby pond saw flames engulfing one of the plant's units.

What followed the blast was a series of heroic and not-so-heroic deeds. On the one hand, firefighters were on site within five minutes of the blast, sacrificing their lives and jumping into unknown danger. On the other hand, the power station's managers and the country's leaders adamantly refused to believe the facts and kept the accident under wraps.

Many firefighters who somehow managed to subdue the fire died shortly after the accident. They had been virtually unprotected and were exposed to radiation so high that saving them was impossible.

chapters.title: Truth has a way of leaking out
chapters.text:
After the plant's management and party leaders in Kyiv and Moscow realized what had happened, their main task was to keep things as quiet as possible and avoid panic among the public. They were so afraid of the accident going public that they delayed the evacuation of Pripyat's population. People felt, though, that something was wrong on a deeply intuitive level, and some began to leave the city long before the official evacuation started.

When the government finally evacuated them, most had already been exposed to the radiation for far too long for it to go unnoticed. Many paid the full price for the two days spent unaware of the danger. Some died very young, and others carried severe health issues for the rest of their lives.

Even when thousands of people were evacuated, the Soviet government refused to issue a public statement about what had happened. The Kremlin had quite a history of keeping nuclear accidents under wraps. This time, however, hiding the truth was much harder. The information spread with the wind, both metaphorically and literally. The Chornobyl blast released fifty million curies of radiation that spread over Europe and even reached Sweden. Some countries had already detected that something was wrong and were demanding answers.

The secret was out in the open, and an international scandal was brewing, as Serhii Plokhy has written.

Soviet media finally made a statement on the evening of April 28, 1986, almost three days after the blast. The message was short and downplayed the gravity of the situation, but it set off a series of responses from other world leaders. Ronald Reagan, then president of the United States, offered help in dealing with the situation.

Because the facts were scarce, disinformation spread quickly, and media around the world reported contradictory data and figures. The whole world was concerned, everyone but the Soviet leaders.

Not knowing is the primary source of public fear and panic.

The world reacted harshly to the Soviet Union's handling of the situation. In a paradox, Mikhail Gorbachev's first speech about the catastrophe criticized the West's reaction.

Even so, the Chornobyl tragedy transformed Soviet media. Though the change was small and reluctant, it marked a step toward openness.

chapters.title: Cleaning up after a disaster of this scale is grueling
chapters.text:
What happened on April 26, 1986, had a long list of consequences. The danger did not stop once the fire did. The contamination spread in other ways. The first step toward managing the nuclear fallout was creating an exclusion zone. There were many debates about it, but after surprisingly thorough research and a series of consultations, the Soviets decided that the zone should have a radius of thirty kilometers. Because of that decision, they had to evacuate an additional forty thousand people. The experience was traumatic for the evacuees, who did not fully grasp the dangers of their invisible enemy.

The contamination process created a new grim reality. When planning the work, crews had to calculate how long they could be exposed to radiation and how many lives would be lost. The people actively dealing with the blast's aftermath later became known as liquidators, men and women from all over the country who became the heroes of the time. But hazards were everywhere in the new exclusion zone, creating an unsuitable living environment.

Roughly six hundred thousand liquidators worked in the exclusion zone. In the decades that followed, disability and death rates continued to peak among these workers.

By 1989, the contamination was, for the most part, under control. Three of the four plant units continued to operate, but the consequences of the Chornobyl tragedy were far from over. Apart from the obvious and tragic ecological effects, there were political ones as well.

What happened at Chornobyl cracked the very foundations of the USSR. The days of the Soviet Union were almost over, and a new political era was about to begin. Ukraine, the country that had suffered the most from the nuclear blast, was about to become the first of the Soviet republics to break free.

By the end of 1989, after a series of publications and protests mainly organized by the Ukrainian movement Rukh, Ukraine and neighboring Belarus rose up against the Soviet authorities. They despised Soviet secrecy and lies. In the next elections, both countries chose candidates who were prominent supporters of independence.

Discovering the unembellished truth can set you free.

By learning the truth about Chornobyl and its dangers, other nations once locked under the regime found the power to break free. On August 24, 1991, Ukraine declared its independence from the USSR. Hopes ran high among people who expected things to change. Everyone was tired of all-encompassing communism. Later, the former Soviet officials who had dealt with the Chornobyl catastrophe faced severe repercussions.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
The city of Pripyat and the surrounding exclusion zone feel like a time capsule. It is one of the few places in Ukraine where you can still see the original communist symbols and feel the real-life impact of the tragedy. Despite more than thirty years passing since the blast, the environment still feels as apocalyptic as it did on April 26, 1986.

Chornobyl became a monumental symbol as well. Even though the days of the Soviet Union are over, it reminds us of the long list of failures, sacrifices, and needless suffering that this state inflicted on its people. The tragedy hit ordinary people the hardest, while the leaders tried to escape any accountability for their inaction. The Chornobyl nuclear station is a place frozen in time, and its presence reminds us of both the dangers and the merits of technological progress.

On the surface, the Chornobyl tragedy was simply a test that went wrong, setting off a tragic chain reaction. On a deeper level, the roots of the catastrophe reach back to the flawed Soviet system and the nuclear arms race it took part in. Whether or not a similar tragedy unfolds in the near future is the shared responsibility of the whole world. We have to learn from history and revise our lessons.

Try this. Watch the acclaimed historical TV mini-series Chernobyl if you want to learn more about the events surrounding the blast through a cinematic lens. If you wish, book an online or offline tour of the exclusion zone.
